content.text:
*"You did not choose me, but I chose you and appointed 
you so that you might go and bear fruit."*{John 15:16}{When order does not matter, we count combinations; when it does, we count permutations.}{Blaise Pascal}

A **combination** is a selection of objects where 
order does not matter.

content.title: Combination
content.content:
An **$r$-combination** of $n$ objects is an unordered selection of 
$r$ of the $n$ objects. The number of $r$-combinations is the binomial 
coefficient:
\[ \binom{n}{r} = C(n, r) = \frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!} = \frac{P(n, r)}{r!} \]

content.text:
We divide by $r!$ because each combination of $r$ objects can be arranged 
in $r!$ ways, all of which we've counted as separate permutations.

content.title: Committee selection
content.problem: How many ways can a 3-person committee be chosen from 10 people?

content.title: Card hands
content.problem: How many 5-card hands can be dealt from a standard 52-card deck?

content.content:
Consider selecting $r$ items from $n$. Fix one item $x$.

Either $x$ is selected: choose remaining $r-1$ from $n-1$: $\binom{n-1}{r-1}$ ways.

Or $x$ is not selected: choose all $r$ from remaining $n-1$: $\binom{n-1}{r}$ ways.

Total: $\binom{n-1}{r-1} + \binom{n-1}{r} = \binom{n}{r}$.

content.title: Combinations with Repetition
content.content:
The number of ways to select $r$ objects from $n$ types *with 
repetition allowed* is:
\[ \binom{n + r - 1}{r} = \binom{n + r - 1}{n - 1} \]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Fruit selection
content.problem:
A store has apples, oranges, and pears. How many ways can you select 5 
pieces of fruit?

marginNotes.text:
And we know that in all things God works 
for the good of those who love Him, who have been called according to 
His purpose.
